fact: October 11 is exactly 284 days into the year, which means there are only 81 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, squirrels are typically frantically burying nuts around this time - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season and forget where half of them are!
The sun is setting about 2 minutes earlier each day around October 11 in northern regions, making the days shrink faster than a deflating balloon.
Maple trees in North America are often pumping out their brightest red and orange colors right now because they're basically having a sugar rush - all that stored sugar creates the brilliant colors!
October 11 falls in the middle of peak aurora season in polar regions, when the northern lights often dance across the sky on clear nights.
Monarch butterflies are usually flying over 2,000 miles south around this time, and some passing by today won't stop until they reach the mountains of Mexico!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, October 11 is typically prime baby animal season - kangaroo joeys are peeking out of pouches and penguin chicks are learning to swim!
Morning frost often starts appearing on grass around this date in northern climates, creating millions of tiny ice crystals that look like nature's glitter bomb exploded overnight.
The moon often looks extra huge and orange when it rises around October 11 because of particles floating in the autumn air - it's like looking through a giant Instagram filter!
Geese flying south around this time can reach speeds of 40 mph and fly for 16 hours straight without stopping - that's like running a marathon while flying through the clouds!
